title: Mesoscopic Josephson effect in graphene disk at magnetic field
abstract: Unlike for tunneling Josephson junctions, for which the current-phase relation is given by the sine function, with the critical current ($I_c$) and normal-state resistance ($R_N$) following the relation $I_cR_N=(π/2)\,Δ_0/e$ (where $Δ_0$ is the superconducting gap and electron charge is $-e$), mesoscopic Josephson junctions show more complex current-phase relations, with the skewness $S>0$, what is related to the presence -- in case the leads are in the normal state -- of transmission probabilities taking the values comparable to $1$. Here, we show that these features also appear for a superconductor-graphene-superconductor (S-g-S) junction in the disk-shaped (Corbino) geometry, when the magnetic field is adjusted such that $I_c\rightarrow{}0$ and $R_N\rightarrow{}\infty$. In such a case, the product $I_cR_N\approx{}1.85\,Δ_0/e$, and the skewness $S\approx{}0.14$. The results obtained from quantum-mechanical mode-matching analysis for the Dirac-Bogoliubov-De-Gennes equation are compared with simpler model assuming incoherent scattering between two circular interfaces separating the sample and the leads.
summary: This paper explores how the geometry and magnetic field of a graphene Corbino disk influence the Josephson effect. It demonstrates that even in the limit of very high resistance, the junction maintains mesoscopic features like an enhanced I_cR_N product and a skewed current-phase relation. The study provides a detailed comparison between exact quantum-mechanical calculations and simpler scattering models. These findings highlight the persistence of non-trivial transport properties in graphene-based superconducting junctions.
main_problem: Investigating the mesoscopic Josephson effect in a graphene Corbino disk under a perpendicular magnetic field, specifically looking for deviations from the standard tunneling-limit current-phase relation.
main_result: In the limit of vanishing conductance, the system exhibits an enhanced I_cR_N product (~1.85 Delta_0/e) and positive skewness (S ~ 0.14) compared to the standard tunneling limit.
method: Quantum-mechanical mode-matching analysis using the Dirac-Bogoliubov-De-Gennes equation, compared against an incoherent scattering approximation.
model_or_system: A superconductor-graphene-superconductor (S-g-S) junction in a Corbino (disk) geometry, featuring an annular graphene sample with two circular superconducting electrodes.
key_observables: Critical current (I_c), normal-state resistance (R_N), current-phase relation (CPR), skewness (S), and conductance (G).
important_parameters: Magnetic field (B), chemical potential (mu), radii ratio (a), and the short-junction limit (junction width much smaller than coherence length).
main_assumptions: Short-junction limit (width << coherence length); use of an incoherent scattering approximation for comparison; averaging datasets to mitigate Landau level resonance effects.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the Corbino geometry and the current-phase relation; Fig 2 displays conductance, I_cR_N, and skewness as functions of chemical potential; Fig 3 shows the relationship between chemical potential and magnetic field-induced resonances and compares averaged results to incoherent scattering predictions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Corbino geometry, presents the theoretical framework (DBdG equation), details the numerical mode-matching method, compares results with an incoherent scattering model, and analyzes the evolution of Josephson observables under varying magnetic fields and doping.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) Schematic of a graphene disk with inner radius r1 and outer radius r2, contacted by two circular superconducting elec- trodes (yellow areas). A current source drives a dissipationless su- percurrent through the annular region. The uniform magnetic field B = (0, 0, B) is perpendicular to the sample. A separate gate electrode (not shown) allows one to tune the carrier concentration. (b) At low field but high doping, such that the cyclotron diameter 2rc > r2 −r1, incoherent scattering along the classical trajectory governs the transport. (c) Current-phase relation for high doping (|µ| ≫ℏvF /r1) and the narrow-disk limit (r2/r1 →1). Red line correspond to the B = 0 case (see Ref....

Figure 2: (a) Normal-state conductance 1/RN, (b) product IcRN, and (c) skewness of the current-phase relation S as functions of the chemical potential for different magnetic fields, B⋆≡eBr2 1/2ℏ, specified for each line in (a); same line-color encoding is used in (a)–(c). The radii ratio is r2/r1 = 2 (a = 0.5). Dashed-black lines correspond to the B →0, |µ| ≫ℏvF /r1 limit (see Ref. [26]).

Figure 3: (a) Chemical potential corresponding to the activation voltage (µon ≡eUon/2) for the normal state displayed as function of the magnetic field for the two selected threshold conductances: Gmin(Uon) = ηg0, with η = 0.5, 1 [solid lines]. Additional line [dashed] marks 2rc = r2−r1 (or µ⋆ on = B⋆). (b) Product IcRN and (c) skewness S calculated at µ = µon [solid lines]. Thin horizontal lines in (b,c) mark the predictions for incoherent scattering (see also Table I). Insets in (b,c) depict the averages taken for the consecutive intervals between the maxima, i.e., (1 . . . 2), . . . , (5 . . . 6).
